The partial disarmament of Iran-backed Iraqi militias offers a potential off-ramp for one pressure vector, but it faces opposition from hardline factions. The Israel-Gaza conflict continues to entrench, with permanent military infrastructure signaling long-term occupation. All three theaters—Iran, Ukraine, Gaza—remain locked in escalatory cycles with no clear diplomatic resolution in sight.

item.title: Strategic Brief: US-Iran Deal in Doubt as Rubio Rules Out Sanctions Relief for Hormuz Reopening
item.description:
Secretary of State Marco Rubio told Congress the US will not trade sanctions relief for access to the Strait of Hormuz, signaling a harder line as Iran's Fars News reports diplomatic communications have halted. Meanwhile, Russia struck Kyiv with a massive drone and missile barrage after a week of threats, and Ukraine's battlefield momentum continues to build.
► US Rejects Sanctions-for-Hormuz Framework
Secretary of State Marco Rubio told the Senate Foreign Relations Committee on Tuesday that the United States is not offering Iran sanctions relief in exchange for reopening the Strait of Hormuz. Testifying in his first congressional appearance since the Iran war began on February 28, Rubio said that any sanctions relief would be tied exclusively to Tehran giving up its nuclear program. The comments, reported by Reuters and Al-Monitor, represent a significant hardening of the US negotiating position.Rubio defended the US military campaign as having degraded Iran's conventional military capabilities, noting that Tehran 'no longer has the conventional shield to hide behind.' He said an interim deal could 'happen today; it could happen tomorrow; it could happen next week,' but acknowledged there is no guarantee negotiations will produce an agreement acceptable to the Senate or the American public. 'The first thing that is a predicate to anything else happening—the straits have to be reopened,' Rubio told lawmakers.The secretary also confirmed that Iran's new Supreme Leader, Mojtaba Khamenei, is 'increasingly engaging at some level' through intermediaries, despite not being seen publicly. Mojtaba succeeded his father Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, who was killed on the first day of US-Israeli strikes on February 28. Rubio's characterization contradicted earlier reports that the younger Khamenei was unconscious and receiving treatment in Qom.
Why it matters: The explicit rejection of sanctions-for-Hormuz narrows the diplomatic space for a near-term agreement. Iran has charged ships passage fees through the strait and demanded an end to Israeli operations in Lebanon as preconditions. By ruling out sanctions relief for Hormuz access, the US is betting that military pressure has weakened Tehran enough to extract concessions on nuclear issues.
► Iran Suspends Message Exchange with US
Iran's semi-official Fars News agency reported Tuesday that the exchange of messages between Tehran and Washington via mediators stopped several days ago, contradicting President Donald Trump's assertion on Monday that talks with Iran were progressing 'at a rapid pace.' Fars quoted an unnamed 'informed source' saying Iran's last message to Washington conveyed a clear stance regarding its demand for an end to Israel's war with Hezbollah in Lebanon as part of any US-Iran agreement.The same day, Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) said it targeted the MSC Sariska V cargo ship in retaliation for a US strike on an Iranian vessel in the Sea of Oman. The IRGC claimed the vessel was American-Israeli owned; the shipping company MSC denied this, stating the ship was Italian-owned and that all crew members were safe. The IRGC also confirmed that 24 vessels transited the Strait of Hormuz in the past 24 hours after obtaining authorization, and said more than 300 non-Iranian vessels have applied for safe passage since early May.A senior IRGC commander, Brigadier General Mohammad Jafar Asadi, said Iran has not yet 'revealed all our trump cards' and warned that 'when surrender is not an option, war lies ahead.' The statements suggest Iran is preparing for a prolonged standoff rather than near-term accommodation.
Why it matters: The suspension of diplomatic messaging and continued IRGC maritime operations indicate that Tehran sees leverage in maintaining pressure rather than rushing to close a deal. The IRGC's control over Hormuz transit and willingness to target commercial shipping reinforces that leverage, even as the US maintains a naval blockade.
► Russia Strikes Kyiv After Week of Threats
Russia launched hundreds of drones and dozens of missiles at Kyiv early Tuesday, killing at least 16 people according to Ukrainian authorities. The attack came more than a week after Moscow warned of a strike so large that diplomats and foreigners should flee the capital. The New York Times reported that Russian aircraft had spent days mimicking large attack formations, setting off alarms and wearing down residents before the actual strike arrived.The bombardment matched the scale of two other deadly attacks on Kyiv last month, but analysts noted a psychological dimension: Russia's foreign ministry had issued dire warnings about retaliation for a Ukrainian drone strike that killed 21 students in the Russian-controlled Luhansk region. The delay between threats and strikes appeared designed to inflict a psychological toll, with Western envoys publicly vowing to stay in Kyiv to avoid signaling intimidation.The attacks come at a difficult moment for Russia's war effort. According to a New York Times analysis, Moscow's advance in Ukraine has slowed nearly to a halt, with coerced mobilization in occupied territories and domestic recruitment falling short. Russia is stepping up coerced recruitment in occupied eastern Ukraine as its domestic efforts falter, and domestic discontent is reportedly growing.
Why it matters: Russia's strikes on Kyiv demonstrate Moscow's continued ability to inflict pain on Ukrainian civilians, but they also reflect a war effort under strain. The combination of maximal threats and delayed strikes suggests the Kremlin is trying to compensate for battlefield losses with psychological warfare, while domestic pressures mount.
► Ukraine's Momentum Builds as Russia's Falters
Ukraine's military position has strengthened significantly in recent months, according to multiple analysts. Franz-Stefan Gady, a Vienna-based military analyst, told The New York Times that 'Ukraine's position is much, much more formidable now than just a year ago.' Jack Watling of the Royal United Services Institute wrote in Foreign Affairs that 'in Kyiv, there is a growing optimism that Ukraine can fight Russia to a cease-fire,' noting that 'Russian combat performance is waning' despite constant drone strikes and shelling.A detailed analysis in The i Paper identified a pivotal moment: Elon Musk's decision in February to block Russian access to Starlink satellites. The move disrupted communications and drone coordination across large sections of the front, with Ukrainian officials reporting a sharp drop in Russian bombardments on frontline positions. In the first five days after the Starlink cutoff, Ukrainian forces reportedly liberated more than 200 square kilometers—roughly equivalent to Russia's gains for all of December, according to the Institute for the Study of War.Russia's casualty rate has reportedly escalated to over 1,000 soldiers per day in May, a level analysts described as unsustainable. Meanwhile, the European Union ratified a €90 billion loan for Ukraine, Britain agreed to supply 120,000 drones, and Ukraine continues its campaign of strikes on Russian energy infrastructure, forcing fuel rationing in the Moscow region.
Why it matters: The convergence of Ukrainian tactical innovation, Western financial support, and Russian logistical struggles has created the most favorable position for Kyiv in over a year. The Starlink decision illustrates how third-party technology policy can reshape battlefield dynamics, while the EU loan and British drone deal provide Kyiv with resources to sustain operations.
► Israel-Hezbollah Ceasefire Collapses Despite Trump Claim
Fighting between Israel and Hezbollah has continued despite President Trump's statement on Monday that he had persuaded both sides to halt attacks. CBS News reported that Israeli strikes in southern Lebanon killed eight people Tuesday, including a father and his two children. The Israeli military issued evacuation warnings for the southern Lebanese city of Nabatieh hours before peace talks between Israeli and Lebanese officials resumed in Washington.Hezbollah has continued to strike northern Israel, with the group reporting 21 strikes against Israeli forces over a 24-hour period. Israeli public broadcaster Kan estimated that Hezbollah's drone capabilities are now limiting 80% of Israeli troop assaults in southern Lebanon. Lebanon's President Joseph Aoun said Tuesday that 'there is no option other than negotiation' to end the war, calling it 'the duty of the state to take care of its citizens.'The disconnect between diplomatic claims and ground reality highlights the difficulty of separating the Israel-Hezbollah conflict from the broader US-Iran negotiations. Iran has demanded an end to Israeli operations in Lebanon as a precondition for any agreement with Washington, while Israel has continued to expand its campaign despite the ceasefire framework.
Why it matters: The Israel-Hezbollah fighting directly complicates US-Iran diplomacy, as Tehran frames Israeli operations as violations of the broader ceasefire. The continuation of strikes despite Trump's claim that both sides agreed to stop suggests that neither Israel nor Hezbollah is fully aligned with Washington's diplomatic timeline.
► Baku Energy Week Highlights Southern Corridors to Europe
Global energy leaders gathered in Baku for Energy Week 2026 emphasized that southern energy corridors are set to dominate supplies to Europe as traditional routes face disruption. The focus comes as the Strait of Hormuz blockade continues to affect global oil and gas flows, with Iran charging fees for authorized transit.Azerbaijan's Energy Minister Parviz Shahbazov held high-level meetings with EU officials to review strategic cooperation across key energy sectors. The discussions reflect Europe's accelerating effort to diversify energy sources away from Russian gas and Middle Eastern routes vulnerable to conflict. The first meeting of the Azerbaijan-US Economic Dialogue also took place during the forum.The emphasis on southern corridors—from the Caspian through Turkey to Europe—illustrates how regional conflicts are reshaping global energy infrastructure. With Hormuz constrained and Russian gas flows diminished, alternative routes through the South Caucasus and Eastern Mediterranean have gained strategic importance.
Why it matters: Europe's energy diversification drive creates new geopolitical alignments linking the Caspian region to European markets. The Baku forum demonstrates how energy infrastructure decisions made during the current conflicts will shape trade relationships and security dependencies for years to come.
■ The Bottom Line The US-Iran negotiations are at a breaking point. Rubio's categorical rejection of sanctions relief for Hormuz access signals that Washington believes military pressure has given it leverage to demand nuclear concessions rather than a simple trade. Iran's suspension of diplomatic messaging and continued IRGC operations suggest Tehran calculates it can wait out American patience and exploit Israeli actions in Lebanon as a pretext. The simultaneous collapse of the Israel-Hezbollah ceasefire underscores that these conflicts cannot be separated. Meanwhile, Ukraine's position has markedly improved—Russian Starlink losses, unsustainable casualties, and Ukrainian innovation have shifted momentum. The EU loan and British drone deal provide Kyiv resources, while Moscow's strikes on Kyiv demonstrate its continued ability to inflict civilian pain but also reveal a war effort under strain.

item.title: Strategic Brief: Iran Suspends US Peace Talks as Israel Expands Lebanon Offensive
item.description:
Tehran halted negotiations with Washington after Israel widened its Lebanon campaign, threatening to open new fronts and sending oil prices surging. Meanwhile, Ukraine's drone campaign squeezes Moscow's fuel supply, and India races to finalize a trade pact before US tariffs spike.
► Iran Suspends Peace Negotiations with US
Iran's negotiating team suspended all dialogue with US mediators on Monday, according to the semi-official Tasnim news agency, citing Israel's expanding offensive in Lebanon as a breach of the ceasefire's core conditions. Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi declared that 'the ceasefire between Iran and the US is unequivocally a ceasefire on all fronts, including in Lebanon,' framing any Israeli operations there as a violation of the broader truce. The suspension came hours after a brief exchange of strikes between US and Iranian forces, with Iran targeting the base from which a US attack originated.The move jeopardizes weeks of delicate diplomacy brokered by Pakistan and other intermediaries. Oil markets responded immediately: Brent crude surged past $97 per barrel, climbing more than 7% as traders priced in renewed risks to the Strait of Hormuz. Iranian officials and Tasnim explicitly discussed 'completely blocking' Hormuz and opening fronts at Bab el-Mandeb off Yemen, where Iran's Houthi allies could disrupt Suez Canal traffic. The war, launched by the US and Israel on February 28, has already caused global economic disruption through earlier Hormuz closures.
Why it matters: The suspension removes a diplomatic off-ramp from a regional war that has already sent energy prices soaring. If Tehran follows through on threats to Hormuz and Bab el-Mandeb, global oil supply disruptions would intensify, with cascading effects on inflation, trade, and growth.
► Israel Expands Lebanon Offensive to Beirut's Edge
Israel's military ordered evacuations for Beirut's southern suburb of Dahiyeh on Monday, with Defense Minister Israel Katz warning there would be 'no calm in Beirut' if Hezbollah continued its attacks on northern Israel. Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu instructed strikes on 'terrorist targets' in the capital's densely populated southern district. The Israeli army declared all areas south of the Zahrani River—roughly 40 kilometers from the border, including Tyre and Nabatieh—as 'combat zones,' issuing new evacuation orders for towns north of the Litani River.Hezbollah reported 21 strikes against Israeli forces over 24 hours, including drone attacks that the Israeli military confirmed killed one soldier and wounded three others. Israeli public broadcaster Kan estimated that Hezbollah's drone capabilities are now limiting 80% of Israeli troop assaults in southern Lebanon. The expansion to Beirut revives fears for thousands of displaced residents who had only begun returning after the April ceasefire.
Why it matters: The shift from southern Lebanon to Beirut marks a significant escalation that threatens to reignite full-scale conflict and further strains the Iran-US ceasefire framework.
► Ukraine's Refinery Campaign Forces Fuel Rationing in Moscow
Ukrainian drone strikes on Russian oil infrastructure have created fuel shortages that have now reached the Moscow region. According to The Moscow Times, gas stations in Novaya Moskva began limiting gasoline sales to 60 liters per customer and diesel to 100 liters. Similar rationing has been imposed in Russian-occupied Crimea and parts of Ukraine. Russia also banned jet fuel exports from June 1 through November 30 to stabilize domestic supplies.Bloomberg calculations show Ukraine attacked Russian refineries 16 times in May alone, with over 10 additional strikes on pipelines and storage facilities. By mid-May, Ukrainian officials said 10 refineries had been hit, forcing six to suspend operations and reducing Russian refining capacity by roughly 10%. While higher global oil prices from the Iran conflict have boosted Russian revenues—reaching an estimated €734 million per day in April—the Institute for the Study of War estimates repair costs and subsidies have offset much of the gain, costing Moscow approximately $4.7 billion in April alone.
Why it matters: Ukraine's asymmetric campaign is creating direct costs for Russian civilians and degrading Moscow's ability to benefit from war-driven oil prices, demonstrating how drone warfare can target an adversary's economic base.
► South Korea's Exports Hit Four-Decade High on AI Chip Boom
South Korea's exports grew at the strongest annual rate in over four decades in May, driven by record chip sales from a global surge in AI investment. The trade-reliant economy posted growth not seen since the early 1980s, with semiconductor exports hitting all-time highs. Shares in Samsung Electronics and LG Electronics rallied Monday ahead of expected meetings between Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang and Korean tech executives, signaling potential tie-ups in AI and robotics.The record performance comes despite broader global headwinds from the Iran-Israel war and trade tensions. South Korea's success illustrates how AI-driven demand is creating pockets of economic strength even as geopolitical risks weigh on other sectors.
Why it matters: The AI boom is creating a bifurcated global economy: tech-intensive exporters like South Korea are hitting record highs while energy-importing nations face inflation and currency pressure from the Middle East conflict.
► India-US Trade Talks Enter Final Phase
A US Trade Representative team led by chief negotiator Brendan Lynch arrived in Delhi on June 1 for four days of negotiations aimed at finalizing an interim trade agreement. The urgency stems from a new US tariff framework under Section 301 set to take effect July 24, following a Supreme Court ruling that deemed previous reciprocal tariffs illegal. The US has reduced tariffs from 50% in August 2025 to 18% in February 2026, but Indian officials say the current 10% across-the-board rate remains preferable to accepting certain US conditions affecting oil purchases and agricultural imports.US officials, including USTR Jamieson Greer and Ambassador Sergio Gor, have expressed optimism, with Gor saying only '1% of the deal remains.' But India's rupee has weakened past Rs 95 per dollar, and FDI inflows of $94.53 billion in 2025-26 translated to net FDI of only $7.65 billion, reflecting capital outflows. Finalizing the trade pact is seen as critical to restoring investor confidence.
Why it matters: With the Iran war pressuring India's currency and energy costs, Delhi has strong incentives to close the deal—but not at the cost of sovereignty over oil purchases. The outcome will signal whether US-India ties can weather economic nationalism on both sides.
► France Seizes Russia-Linked 'Shadow Fleet' Tanker
French President Emmanuel Macron announced Monday that the French Navy, backed by the UK, intercepted a sanctioned tanker linked to the Russian oil trade in the Atlantic Ocean. The vessel, named Tagor, was boarded Sunday and ordered to head for the French mainland. Russia condemned the action as 'international piracy' and illegal.The seizure marks the latest escalation in enforcement actions against Russia's 'shadow fleet'—vessels operating outside Western insurance and tracking systems to move Russian oil despite sanctions. European nations have intensified interdictions as Moscow seeks to circumvent price caps and export restrictions.
Why it matters: The enforcement action demonstrates continued Western resolve to squeeze Russian oil revenue, but also risks further confrontation with Moscow as the Ukraine war grinds on.
■ The Bottom Line Iran's suspension of peace talks represents the most serious diplomatic rupture since the February war began, removing a potential path to de-escalation at a moment when Israel is widening its Lebanon campaign. Tehran's explicit threats to Hormuz and Bab el-Mandeb send oil prices toward $100, compounding inflationary pressure worldwide. Ukraine's refinery strikes are creating real costs inside Russia, forcing fuel rationing near Moscow and degrading Moscow's ability to benefit from higher global prices. The India-US trade talks and South Korea's export surge illustrate two divergent economic trajectories: one driven by AI investment, the other by geopolitical pressure to reduce vulnerabilities before tariff deadlines hit.

item.title: Strategic Brief: Ceasefire Extension Tempers Hormuz Crisis as Taiwan Frets Over US Arms Delay
item.description:
A 60-day ceasefire extension between the US and Iran has eased immediate tensions in the Strait of Hormuz, but key questions remain unanswered — including whether Iran will retain de facto control of the waterway. Meanwhile, delays in a $14 billion US arms sale to Taiwan and Tomahawk shipments to Japan are raising doubts about US reliability in the Indo-Pacific.
► US-Iran Ceasefire Extension Calms Markets, Not Strategic Questions
US and Iranian negotiators reached a 60-day memorandum of understanding to extend their fragile ceasefire and begin negotiations over Iran's nuclear program, according to CNBC reporting citing US sources. Brent crude fell 58 cents to close at $93.71 per barrel on Thursday after the news broke, erasing earlier gains triggered by fresh military strikes.The extension comes after Iran launched ballistic missiles toward Kuwait on Thursday, which US Central Command said were successfully intercepted. The attack followed US strikes against an Iranian military site believed to threaten American troops and commercial shipping through the Strait of Hormuz. Several Iranian drones were also intercepted, CNBC reported.Iranian state television claimed Wednesday that Tehran had agreed in a draft MOU to reopen the Strait of Hormuz to prewar commercial traffic levels — but under joint Iran-Oman management. The White House dismissed that report as a 'complete fabrication,' with Trump stating that 'no nation will control shipping through the strait.' Yet former Biden energy advisor Amos Hochstein told CNBC that regional leaders already believe Iran has effectively taken control of Hormuz regardless of what any agreement says.Secretary of State Marco Rubio said Wednesday that Trump prefers diplomacy and will give talks 'every chance to succeed.' But the fundamental gaps between US demands (uranium destruction, toll-free passage) and Iran's position remain unresolved. Citigroup noted that oil markets are finding firmer footing as investors price out worst-case scenarios — but cautioned that uncertainty over timing is keeping central banks on alert for energy-driven inflation risks.
Why it matters: The extension buys time but does not resolve the core dispute. Iran's claim to control Hormuz traffic, even if denied by Washington, reflects a reality on the ground: Tehran has demonstrated it can disrupt global oil flows at will. Whether the 60-day period produces a lasting agreement or simply delays the next confrontation depends on whether either side is willing to make genuine concessions — something neither has shown willingness to do.
► Taiwan Arms Sale Delay Fuels Doubts About US Commitment
A proposed $14 billion US arms sale to Taiwan remains in limbo, with President Trump stating that the package is 'under consideration' and suggesting it could be used as a 'negotiating chip' with Beijing, according to an ISW analysis citing Reuters. The package includes Patriot surface-to-air missile systems, NASAMS, and interceptor munitions — critical components for Taiwan's planned integrated air and missile defense network, known as the 'T-dome.'Taiwanese President William Lai has made T-dome a key priority of his administration. A robust air defense network is essential for Taiwan to withstand Chinese precision strikes preceding any invasion attempt. But the delay is now being weaponized by KMT-aligned media in Taiwan to cast doubt on US support, with outlets suggesting Trump is bowing to Chinese pressure.Reuters reported May 22 that no preparations have been made for a call between Trump and Lai, despite Trump saying May 20 that he would speak to Lai about the sale. US and Taiwanese presidents have not publicly held exchanges since Washington severed diplomatic relations with Taipei in 1979. Further delays or cancellation, the ISW notes, would 'have significant deleterious effects on Taiwanese confidence' in the defense partnership.The timing is sensitive: Taiwan is the only APEC member that does not send its head of government to the annual summit, because Beijing blocks such participation. Yet the PRC allowed Taiwan's Minister Without Portfolio Yang Jen-ni to attend an APEC ministerial meeting in Suzhou on May 22-23 — the most senior Taiwanese official to visit the PRC on official business since 2016, when Beijing cut off exchanges after Tsai Ing-wen's election.
Why it matters: Taiwan's defense planning depends on timely US weapons deliveries. A delay of several years — as happened with previous packages — would leave a gap in air defense precisely when China is accelerating its military modernization. The political impact may be more immediate: KMT politicians can point to the delay as evidence that Taiwan cannot rely on Washington, strengthening the case for cross-strait accommodation with Beijing.
► Japan's Tomahawk Missiles Delayed by Iran War
The United States has warned Japan that a planned delivery of 400 Tomahawk cruise missiles may be delayed by two years due to the Iran war, according to ISW citing Taipei Times. The missiles were initially scheduled for delivery in two shipments completed by 2028; the delay could push completion to 2030.Japan had already begun incorporating Tomahawks into training and was preparing to place them on its Aegis-equipped JS Chokai destroyer. The 1,600-kilometer range of Tomahawks is critical to Japan's evolving defense concept, which is shifting from territorial defense to offensive counterstrike capabilities that can respond to Chinese or North Korean missile threats.Defense expert Tatsumi Yuki told Taipei Times that Japan's defense concept, currently under revision, assumed on-time delivery. PRC state media CCTV cited a defense expert saying the delay would affect Japan's defense plans — part of Beijing's broader narrative that the US is an unreliable ally.The delay comes at a delicate moment for Japan's security policy. Tokyo is revising its National Security Strategy and defense buildup plans, with Tomahawks as a centerpiece. Any gap in capability affects Japan's deterrent posture vis-a-vis China and North Korea.
Why it matters: Japan is one of America's closest allies and a cornerstone of Indo-Pacific security. Weapon delivery delays undermine confidence in the US-Japan alliance precisely when Tokyo is making the politically difficult shift from pacifism to counterstrike doctrine. Beijing is already exploiting the narrative — the question is whether Japan adjusts its defense plans or recalibrates its assumptions about US reliability.
► Ukraine's Robotic Warfare Push Forces Russian Adjustments
CNN reported May 30 that Ukraine's deployment of robotic ground systems is 'redefining the war in Ukraine — and forcing Russia onto the back foot.' The report details how unmanned ground vehicles are increasingly taking on roles previously performed by soldiers, from logistics to reconnaissance to direct combat.According to Ukraine's General Staff, Russian forces lost 1,430 personnel over the past day, along with two tanks, seven armored combat vehicles, 70 artillery systems, five MLRS, one air defense system, four ground robotic systems, and 1,781 drones. The scale of drone losses — 8,549 kamikaze drones deployed by Russia in a single day, according to Ukrainian officials — illustrates how both sides are saturating the battlefield with autonomous systems.ISW's May 30 assessment notes that Ukrainian forces continue to strike Russian logistics and command infrastructure. Ukrainian intermediate-range strike capabilities have created what ISW calls 'the start of a new phase of the war,' with systematic attacks on Russian rear areas disrupting Moscow's ability to sustain offensive operations.The EMPR war report for May 30 documents 291 combat engagements across the front, with the heaviest fighting near Pokrovsk (49 Russian assaults repelled) and in the Huliaipole direction (40 attacks). Ukrainian aviation and artillery struck an enemy command post, three artillery systems, and nine troop concentration areas.
Why it matters: The war in Ukraine has become a laboratory for autonomous and robotic warfare at a scale no other conflict has matched. As both sides lose thousands of drones per day, the lessons learned are being absorbed by militaries worldwide — including China, Iran, and NATO members. The question is whether Ukraine's technological adaptation can offset Russian advantages in manpower and artillery, or whether the war settles into a grinding attrition that neither side can win decisively.
► Gaza Ceasefire at Six Months: Progress Stalled, Needs Unmet
Friday marks six months since Gaza's ceasefire took effect on October 10, 2025 — but the milestone has been overshadowed by the Iran conflict and, according to AP, largely 'lost in the confusion.' While the most intense fighting has stopped, humanitarian conditions remain dire and key provisions of the US 20-point ceasefire plan remain unimplemented.Aid groups released a scorecard Thursday showing conditions have actually deteriorated since the Iran war began. 'During the first two weeks of March 2026, trucks entering Gaza declined by 80%, and the price of basic goods increased dramatically,' five international organizations reported. Medical evacuations have stalled. Most aid still enters through a single, Israeli-controlled border post.The biggest unresolved issue: Hamas has not responded to the Board of Peace's disarmament proposal. A US official said Hamas has not been given a definite deadline but 'patience is not unlimited.' Hamas submitted a counter-offer tying any disarmament to guarantees for a sovereign Palestinian state, a halt to Israel's westward expansion in Gaza, and a complete ceasefire — demands Israel has rejected.AP reports that Israeli attacks have killed 738 people in Gaza in the six months since the ceasefire began. While heaviest fighting has subsided, Israeli forces continue airstrikes and fire on Palestinians near military zones, while militants have carried out shooting attacks on troops.
Why it matters: The Gaza ceasefire was supposed to demonstrate that the Board of Peace model — stopping bombardment, then addressing underlying issues — could work. Six months in, the underlying issues are no closer to resolution. Hamas remains armed and in control of parts of Gaza. Israel controls 58% of the territory, up from 53% under the original ceasefire map. The Board of Peace has not met again since the US-Israel strike on Iran began. Gaza offers a cautionary lesson for any Iran deal: a ceasefire without a political settlement is just a pause, not an ending.
► China's Diplomatic Balancing Act on Taiwan
In an unusual move, the PRC allowed Taiwanese Minister Without Portfolio Yang Jen-ni — also head of Taiwan's Office of Trade Negotiations — to attend an APEC ministerial meeting in Suzhou on May 22-23. Yang is the most senior sitting Taiwanese official to visit the PRC on official business since Beijing cut off official exchanges in 2016 after Tsai Ing-wen's election.According to ISW, the PRC maintains that cross-strait relations must be based on the '1992 Consensus' — an alleged verbal agreement that Taiwan is part of 'China,' without specifying which government represents China. Tsai, current President Lai, and the DPP do not recognize this consensus.Beijing's acceptance of a ministerial-level Taiwanese official is an exception to its escalating campaign to constrain Taiwan's international participation. The PRC has blocked Taiwan from the World Health Assembly every year since 2016, pressured countries to exclude or downgrade Taiwan's participation in international organizations, and financially coerced three African countries to close their airspace to block President Lai's state visit to Eswatini.Yet as APEC host, Beijing chose to allow Yang's participation rather than trigger a crisis. The calculation may reflect Beijing's desire to project stability at a time when the US-China relationship is under strain over Taiwan arms sales and the Iran conflict.
Why it matters: Beijing's approach to Taiwan is not monolithic. It combines pressure (blocking WHO participation, airspace coercion) with selective engagement (APEC participation, potential summitry). The Yang visit shows Beijing can calibrate — but the underlying coercion continues. Taiwan's international space remains constrained by Chinese vetoes in every multilateral venue where statehood is required.
■ The Bottom Line Saturday, May 31: The 60-day US-Iran ceasefire extension has bought both sides time and calmed oil markets — but it has not resolved the core strategic question of who controls the Strait of Hormuz. Iran claims joint management with Oman; the White House calls that a fabrication. Regional actors tell CNBC the reality is simpler: Iran controls Hormuz now, regardless of what any agreement says. In the Indo-Pacific, two US weapons delays — Taiwan's $14 billion air defense package and Japan's 400 Tomahawk missiles — are being exploited by Beijing to sow doubt about American reliability. Taiwan's KMT-aligned media and China's state broadcasters are amplifying the same message: Washington cannot be counted on. Meanwhile, Ukraine's robotic warfare push is forcing Russia to adapt, and Gaza's six-month ceasefire has produced a stalemate where Hamas remains armed, Israel controls 58% of the territory, and humanitarian conditions have deteriorated since the Iran war began. The connecting thread across these theaters: ceasefires and diplomatic agreements are only as good as the follow-through, and follow-through is in short supply.

item.title: Strategic Brief: Trump's Iran Decision Pending as NATO Territory Hit and Indo-Pacific Alliances Reshaped
item.description:
A Russian drone struck a Romanian apartment building, wounding two civilians—the first confirmed casualties on NATO soil from the Ukraine war. Meanwhile, Trump convened a Situation Room meeting on an Iran ceasefire extension but left without announcement, and Defense Secretary Hegseth signaled a transactional new approach to Indo-Pacific allies while notably omitting Taiwan.
► NATO Territory: Russian Drone Hits Romanian Apartment
A Russian drone struck an apartment building in Galati, Romania, on May 29, wounding two civilians—the first confirmed casualties on NATO soil since Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine. Romanian F-16s scrambled in response, and President Nicusor Dan said the alliance stands ready to defend 'every inch' of allied territory. Romania's foreign minister said the strike could justify emergency consultations under Article 4, though no member has invoked Article 5, the collective defense clause.The incident highlights a broader pattern: Russian GPS spoofing from Kaliningrad is steering Ukrainian strike drones off course and into NATO airspace. Lithuania counted 36 spoofing transmitters this week, up from three at the start of 2025, reaching 450 kilometers across the region, according to Reuters. A Ukrainian drone struck a Latvian oil depot on May 7, and a Romanian F-16 shot down another over Estonia on May 19—the first time an allied jet downed a drone believed to be Ukrainian. Ukraine has apologized to Baltic states, blaming Russian electronic warfare for diverting its drones. Lithuanian Defense Minister Robertas Kaunas said the alliance must adapt because 'the possibility of repeated similar scenarios is very high.'
Why it matters: This marks the first confirmed casualties from the Ukraine conflict on NATO territory, testing the alliance's collective defense commitments. The broader pattern of Russian electronic warfare diverting drones into allied airspace creates a persistent escalation risk that NATO has not yet defined a clear response doctrine for.
► Iran Deal: Trump's Situation Room Meeting Ends Without Decision
President Trump convened a two-hour meeting in the White House Situation Room on May 30 to make a 'final determination' on a proposed agreement to extend the U.S.-Iran ceasefire by 60 days, but the White House did not announce a decision. Trump said on Truth Social that any deal must see Iran's highly enriched uranium 'unearthed by the United States' and 'DESTROYED,' and that 'Iran must agree that they will never have a Nuclear Weapon or Bomb.' He also demanded the Strait of Hormuz reopen immediately with 'no tolls' for unrestricted shipping.Iran's semi-official Fars news agency called Trump's claims 'a mixture of truth and lies,' saying the draft agreement includes U.S. acceptance of $12 billion in frozen asset releases and a complete ceasefire in Lebanon—neither mentioned by Trump. Iran's Foreign Ministry said there are 'no negotiations' on nuclear issues, only focus on 'ending the war.' The head of the IAEA, Rafael Grossi, told the Financial Times that Kazakhstan has offered to take Iran's stockpile of roughly 440 kilograms of uranium enriched to 60% purity if a deal is reached.Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth, speaking in Singapore, said talks were 'productive' and Iran is 'coming in our direction,' but also said the military is 'postured even stronger today than we were on day one' if negotiations fail. The war launched February 28 has killed thousands, mainly in Iran and Lebanon, and driven global fuel prices higher.
Why it matters: The gap between U.S. and Iranian framing of the proposed deal is significant—Trump describes nuclear dismantlement and toll-free Hormuz passage, while Iranian sources emphasize frozen asset releases and a Lebanon ceasefire. No decision yet means uncertainty persists for oil markets and regional actors.
► Shangri-La Dialogue: Hegseth's Transactional Indo-Pacific Pitch
Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth addressed the Shangri-La Dialogue in Singapore on May 30, pledging to prioritize 'model allies' that meet U.S. expectations for defense spending—3.5% of GDP—and warning that Washington will move others 'to the front of the line' for expedited arms sales, intelligence sharing, and industrial base collaboration. He listed Australia, India, Indonesia, Japan, Malaysia, the Philippines, South Korea, Singapore, Thailand, and Vietnam as partners 'stepping up.' Taiwan and New Zealand were not mentioned.Hegseth struck a more conciliatory tone on China than in 2025, following the Trump-Xi summit in Beijing two weeks ago that established a framework of 'constructive strategic stability.' He said the U.S. seeks 'a genuinely stable equilibrium' and will act with 'strong, quiet and clear actions.' When asked about arms sales to Taiwan, Hegseth did not answer directly. Taiwan's former defense minister Andrew Yang noted that U.S. support for Taiwan 'can be answered only by President Trump.' Trump had said he was unsure if he would approve what would have been the largest arms sale to Taiwan—worth $14 billion—after the Beijing summit.On the sidelines, Hegseth announced with British and Australian counterparts that AUKUS partners are working together on unmanned undersea vehicles under 'Pillar Two' of the trilateral defense pact, focused on frontier technologies including AI, quantum, and hypersonic capabilities.
Why it matters: The speech signals a tiered alliance structure in the Indo-Pacific where benefits flow to countries that spend more on defense. The omission of Taiwan from a major defense speech at Asia's premier security forum is notable after Trump's uncertainty about the $14 billion arms package, raising questions about the U.S. commitment to the island's defense.
► Middle East Fronts: Lebanon Casualties Mount as Israel Crosses Litani
Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said Friday that Israeli ground forces have crossed Lebanon's Litani River, about 18 miles north of the Israel-Lebanon border. Israel's military declared a large area of southern Lebanon an 'active combat zone' and ordered evacuations. The UN's children's agency UNICEF reported that an average of 11 children have been killed or wounded every day in Lebanon over the past week—7 killed and 30 injured on Thursday alone. Since a disregarded April 17 ceasefire, 55 children have been killed and 212 wounded, according to UNICEF.Doctors Without Borders (MSF) said civilians continue arriving at hospitals with severe injuries including skull fractures, traumatic brain injuries, and shrapnel from drone explosions 'lodged in their lungs and liver.' Lebanon's health ministry reports 3,324 killed and 10,000 wounded since the Hezbollah-Israel war began March 2, with more than 1 million displaced. Israel says 23 soldiers and 4 civilians have been killed.Iran's semi-official Fars news agency said a complete ceasefire in Lebanon 'in accordance with Hezbollah' is part of the draft agreement with the U.S., though Trump did not mention Lebanon in his public comments on the deal.
Why it matters: The fighting in Lebanon continues to exact a heavy civilian toll and has displaced over a million people, even as ceasefire talks proceed. Whether any U.S.-Iran deal includes a Lebanon ceasefire has become a point of contention between American and Iranian public statements.
► Global Economic Impact: Energy Warning from International Organizations
The heads of the International Energy Agency, International Monetary Fund, World Bank, and World Trade Organization issued a joint statement May 29 warning that the Middle East war is straining global energy supplies. The Strait of Hormuz—carrying about one-fifth of the world's oil—has been effectively closed for three months, and 'global oil inventories are being drawn down at a record pace.' They warned of 'increasing risks for fuel security, market conditions, and broader economic resilience' as demand picks up in summer.Brent crude fell 1.8% to $92 per barrel Friday on optimism about a potential Iran deal extension, but remains well above the $70 level before the war began in late February. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said the U.S. has seized roughly $1 billion in Iranian cryptocurrency assets. Meanwhile, the U.S. military stopped a sixth merchant vessel trying to break the blockade of Iranian ports, disabling the Gambia-flagged Lian Star in the Gulf of Oman after it ignored multiple warnings.
Why it matters: The joint statement from major international economic bodies underscores that the Hormuz closure is no longer a regional issue but a systemic risk to the global economy. Even a 60-day ceasefire extension may not immediately restore normal tanker traffic given the need for mine clearance and security arrangements.
■ The Bottom Line A Russian drone strike on Romanian civilian infrastructure—the first confirmed casualties on NATO soil from the Ukraine war—tests the alliance's response threshold amid a wider pattern of Russian electronic warfare pushing drones into allied airspace. In Washington, Trump's Iran decision meeting ended without announcement, leaving the gap between U.S. and Iranian framing of the proposed deal unresolved. Hegseth's Shangri-La speech signaled a transactional alliance model for the Indo-Pacific, rewarding defense spending with expedited arms sales while omitting Taiwan from a list of partners. The humanitarian toll in Lebanon continues to mount, and international economic bodies warn of systemic fuel security risks if Hormuz remains closed through summer demand peaks.

item.title: Strategic Brief: The Deal Edges Closer: Pakistan's Mediation Push and NATO's Identity Crisis
item.description:
US-Iran talks show 'slight progress' as Pakistan's army chief heads to Tehran, while NATO allies face confusion over American troop cuts and Europe confronts the reality of higher energy prices through 2027.
► Pakistan's Mediation Intensifies as Tehran Weighs US Proposal
Pakistan's Army Chief Field Marshal Asim Munir departed for Tehran on Friday, the second visit in just over a month, as Islamabad ramps up efforts to broker a peace deal between the US and Iran. Pakistani Interior Minister Mohsin Naqvi has been in Tehran since Wednesday, meeting twice with Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi to review proposals for ending the war.US Secretary of State Marco Rubio acknowledged 'slight progress' in talks with Iran on Friday, though he cautioned against exaggerating the movement. 'I don't want to exaggerate it, but there's been a little bit of movement, and that's good,' Rubio told reporters during NATO meetings in Sweden. The cautious optimism follows President Trump's statement that he was holding off on a military strike because 'serious negotiations' were underway.UAE presidential advisor Anwar Gargash assessed the odds of a deal at '50-50,' warning that any settlement must address root causes of instability. The central sticking point remains the Strait of Hormuz, where Iran has established what it calls a 'tolling system' for ship transits — a mechanism Rubio explicitly rejected in Friday's comments.
Why it matters: Pakistan has emerged as the critical mediator in the US-Iran conflict, leveraging its relationships with both Tehran and Washington. The intensity of Pakistani diplomatic engagement — with both the interior minister and army chief in Tehran simultaneously — signals that negotiations have moved beyond procedural discussions into substantive territory. Whether Tehran accepts US terms on Hormuz access will determine if the ceasefire holds or the war resumes.
► EU Sanctions Iran Over Hormuz Blockade as Oil Prices Climb
European Union nations moved Friday toward imposing sanctions on Iranian officials responsible for blocking the Strait of Hormuz, deeming the blockade 'contrary to international law.' The technical step extends the scope of existing Iran sanctions regimes, allowing for travel bans and asset freezes on individuals involved in disrupting freedom of navigation.Oil prices rose Friday as investors doubted the prospects of a breakthrough in peace talks. Market uncertainty persists despite diplomatic signals, with buyers also preparing for the US summer driving season. Prices remain on track for a weekly loss, reflecting the volatile calculus of war and diplomacy.US Central Command reported that American forces have redirected 97 commercial vessels since imposing a military blockade on Iranian ports and ships in mid-April, with four vessels disabled for non-compliance. The dual blockade — Iran at Hormuz, the US at Iranian ports — continues to squeeze global energy flows.
Why it matters: The EU sanctions move formalizes Europe's alignment with US pressure on Iran, though the economic burden falls heavily on European consumers. ECB President Christine Lagarde confirmed that even if the conflict ended now, 'lagging effects' would keep prices elevated through 2027, with inflation forecast at 3.1% this year — significantly higher than earlier projections of 1.9%.
► NATO Allies Confront American Troop Cuts and Communication Chaos
Secretary of State Rubio told NATO allies in Sweden to 'face up' to upcoming US troop cuts in Europe, acknowledging that while allies are 'aware' of the adjustments, they won't be 'thrilled about it.' The meeting in Helsingborg was marked by confusion after Trump announced 5,000 troops would go to Poland — reversing an earlier decision to cancel that deployment — just weeks after abruptly announcing the withdrawal of 5,000 troops from Germany.Swedish Foreign Minister Maria Malmer Stenergard called the situation 'confusing indeed, and not always easy to navigate.' Norwegian FM Espen Barth Eide stressed the importance of structured drawdowns so 'Europe is able to build up when the US reduces its presence.'The diplomatic friction comes after Trump criticized European allies for refusing to support his Iran war and threatened to consider leaving NATO. Rubio reiterated Trump's 'disappointment' with allies and said it would 'have to be addressed.' European nations have dispatched vessels toward the Gulf to help with Hormuz operations when the war ends.
Why it matters: NATO's July summit in Ankara was supposed to showcase European defense spending increases — commitments to reach 5% of GDP on defense. Instead, allies are struggling to coordinate with a US administration that sends mixed signals on troop deployments. France's FM Jean-Noel Barrot called for 'Europeanising NATO,' reflecting growing recognition that Europe must shoulder more of its own security burden. The Iran war has exposed how dependent European energy security remains on US military power.
► Israel-Lebanon Tensions Persist Despite Ceasefire Framework
More than 3,100 people have been killed by Israeli attacks in Lebanon since March 2, according to Lebanon's health ministry, with 9,432 injured. An Israeli strike on Friday killed six people in southern Lebanon, including two rescuers and a child. Attacks continue despite a US-brokered ceasefire announced in mid-April, which Hezbollah has rejected.Lebanon and Israel are preparing for a fourth round of peace talks in Washington in June, with Lebanon forming a military delegation for security discussions at the Pentagon on May 29. The US sanctioned a group of Hezbollah-affiliated parliamentarians and security officials Thursday, including Iran's ambassador to Lebanon, for obstructing disarmament efforts.The Lebanese army issued a statement affirming that all officers remain loyal to the institution after the US sanctions included, for the first time, an army officer accused of sharing information with Hezbollah.
Why it matters: The Lebanon-Israel front remains volatile even as diplomatic frameworks take shape. Hezbollah's rejection of the ceasefire creates a two-track reality: official Lebanese government participation in talks while the militia continues operations. The death toll climbing past 3,100 adds pressure on all sides. The US sanctions on a Lebanese army officer mark a new escalation, testing the military's institutional cohesion at a critical moment.
► Russia-China Summit Signals Deepening Alignment
Vladimir Putin's visit to Beijing on May 19-20, coming just days after Trump's own trip to China, delivered a clear political message: Western pressure has not isolated Russia. Xi Jinping hailed a 'new stage' in China-Russia relations, with joint statements criticizing US missile defense plans and warning against a return to the 'law of the jungle.'Behind the ceremony, however, the partnership's limits remain visible. Russia and China failed to finalize the Power of Siberia 2 pipeline deal, which would carry 50 billion cubic meters of gas annually through Mongolia. Gazprom shares fell after the summit produced no breakthrough and no dividend for 2025.For India, the deepening Russia-China alignment poses a strategic dilemma. Moscow's growing dependence on Beijing could affect defense technology transfers and energy pricing for New Delhi. India's 2026 BRICS chairmanship becomes a platform to define multipolarity in its own terms — not simply as anti-US alignment.
Why it matters: The Russia-China relationship is becoming more institutionalized but remains asymmetrical. China holds the stronger hand: Russia needs Chinese markets, technology, and finance far more than China needs Russia. Yet the partnership serves both: Moscow gains economic lifelines and diplomatic cover, while Beijing acquires discounted energy and a partner willing to challenge US influence. The missing pipeline deal shows that Beijing negotiates hard even with 'friends.'
■ The Bottom Line Three months into the US-Iran war, the diplomatic track shows signs of life but no breakthrough. Pakistan's intensive mediation — now involving both the interior minister and army chief — signals that substantive negotiations are underway. Rubio's 'slight progress' and Gargash's '50-50' odds suggest the gap between US and Iranian positions has narrowed, though whether Tehran accepts terms on Hormuz remains uncertain. Meanwhile, the economic and alliance fallout continues: EU inflation projections revised sharply upward, oil prices volatile on deal skepticism, and NATO allies struggling to parse contradictory signals from Washington on troop deployments. The Russia-China summit in Beijing confirmed a deepening partnership built on shared grievances but limited by Chinese leverage over Moscow. For all the diplomatic movement, nothing has fundamentally changed the structure of the standoff — Iran still holds Hormuz, the US maintains its blockade, and the question remains whether both sides can find a face-saving formula to de-escalate.

item.title: Strategic Brief: Diplomacy's False Floor: Pakistan Mediates US-Iran Talks While Asia Pays the Price
item.description:
Six weeks into the US-Iran ceasefire, Pakistan is scrambling to revive stalled peace talks while the economic damage spreads across Asia and the Middle East. Iran is reviewing Washington's latest proposal, but neither side is bending — and the countries bearing the costs have no seat at the table.
► Pakistan's Diplomatic Sprint
Pakistan's army chief visited Tehran on Thursday to push US-Iran peace talks back on track. Six weeks after a fragile ceasefire paused the fighting, negotiations have made little progress. Iran is reviewing the latest US proposal, while President Trump says he's willing to wait 'a couple of days' for Tehran's response — a deadline that keeps shifting.The mediation effort reveals the structural problem: Pakistan is carrying diplomatic water for negotiations where it has no leverage over the outcome. Islamabad's army chief is shuttling between Washington and Tehran, but the key decisions sit with capitals that are thousands of miles apart in both geography and objectives. Iran's foreign minister stated clearly that Tehran has 'no trust' in the US and will negotiate only if Washington is 'serious' — a signal that the gap remains wide despite six weeks of talking.
Why it matters: The longer the stalemate persists, the more leverage Iran gains from its de facto control of Hormuz. Every day without resolution normalizes a checkpoint regime that global shipping was never designed to accommodate.
► Asia's Economic Wound Deepens
The damage from Hormuz is distributed unevenly, and Asia is absorbing the heaviest costs. Japan imports 95% of its crude from the Middle East, with three-quarters moving through the strait. The Nikkei 225 fell 8% in the conflict's first two weeks. South Korea sources 68% of its crude through Hormuz — roughly 1.7 million barrels per day. The KOSPI dropped over 11%, with circuit breakers triggered on March 4. Both countries are drawing down strategic reserves, but those reserves are finite.India faces a dual shock: 60% of its oil imports and more than half its natural gas supplies transit Hormuz. Because its LNG contracts are Brent-indexed, the crude spike simultaneously raises oil costs and gas contract prices. Top refining operators have absorbed significant margin compression, while the manufacturing sector faces sustained input cost pressure.Japan has approached Saudi Arabia for emergency supply increases — a signal that even 254 days of strategic crude reserves cannot wait indefinitely. For South Korea, the threat hits energy-intensive export industries like semiconductor fabrication and petrochemicals that took decades to build and cannot easily pivot.
Why it matters: The countries paying the highest price for Hormuz's closure have the least influence over its resolution. Japan and South Korea signed a coalition statement supporting safe passage but declined to send warships. The gap between exposure and agency is now the defining feature of this crisis.
► The Food Security Chain Reaction
The FAO has warned that time is running out to avoid a serious agrifood crisis in the next 6-12 months. The Persian Gulf accounts for 30-35% of global urea exports and 20-30% of global ammonia exports — the chemical inputs that modern agriculture depends on. Qatar halted LNG production at Ras Laffan after Iranian drone strikes, and India subsequently reduced production at three urea plants. Ethiopia's finance minister called for coordinated international action on fuel and fertilizer pressures.The chain reaction is straightforward: fertilizer shortages drive up food production costs, which translate into higher food prices for countries already struggling with inflation. The World Food Programme has warned about deepening hunger in Myanmar, while the EU announced €8 million in emergency food aid. These are early-stage responses to a supply disruption that could last months.
Why it matters: Energy shocks travel through fertilizer into food. The countries least equipped to absorb price spikes — Bangladesh, Pakistan, India, parts of sub-Saharan Africa — are precisely where the fertilizer dependency is highest and the substitution options are fewest.
► Jordan's Collision with a War It Didn't Start
Jordan offers a case study in what happens when geography meets a conflict you did not choose. The kingdom has spent 62 days running an active air defense campaign over its own cities. Iran launched at least 262 missiles and drones at 'vital sites' on Jordanian territory — not projectiles transiting toward Israel, but deliberate strikes on military infrastructure including Muwaffaq Salti Air Base. Civil defense teams logged 414 debris incidents. Fragments landed in residential neighborhoods of Irbid, a city of 800,000 people.The energy shock arrived with equal force. Israel suspended gas exports that had accounted for 85% of Jordan's energy imports and generated 70% of its domestic electricity. The emergency pivot to imported LNG through Aqaba costs an additional $120-140 million per month on a budget already running a $1.5 billion deficit. Tourism — 15% of GDP — collapsed. Petra's cancellations reached 100% in March. April bookings ran 60-65% below projections.Independent economic modeling places Jordan as the single most exposed non-belligerent importer in the world, with a projected 180-day cumulative GDP loss of -6.35% — worse than Lebanon, Singapore, or Egypt. None of those countries are also intercepting missiles over their cities.
Why it matters: Jordan did not launch strikes, sign an alliance, or choose the moment. It is paying in missile fragments, empty hotels, emergency fuel imports, and a fiscal gap with no easy solutions. The distance between Washington's decision to strike and Amman's price tag is the structural asymmetry of modern conflict.
► China's Calculated Distance
Beijing condemned the war and called for de-escalation, extracted bilateral passage rights for Chinese-flagged vessels through Hormuz, declined to contribute to US-led reopening efforts, and positioned itself through existing investments in Southeast Asian renewable energy infrastructure as the responsible long-term alternative to an America whose foreign policy created the crisis Asia is living through.China holds approximately 1.2 billion barrels in strategic crude reserves — roughly 108 days of import cover under zero new inflows. Roughly 40% of its oil imports pass through Hormuz, but its coal-heavy energy mix and domestic production capacity provide buffers that Tokyo and Seoul do not have. More importantly, Iran sells approximately 90% of its crude to China — a commercial dependency that has given Chinese vessels practical passage rights even as Western shipping retreats.
Why it matters: China extracted leverage from a crisis it did not start and has not resolved. By securing passage for its own fleet while declining to help reopen the strait, Beijing demonstrated that the gap between who bears costs and who holds agency is not an accident — it's a feature of how power is distributed in a disrupted system.
► Trump Signals Taiwan Call Despite Xi Warning
President Trump said he plans to speak with Taiwan President Lai Ching-te — a move that would mark the first direct conversation between sitting US and Taiwanese presidents since Washington switched diplomatic recognition from Taipei to Beijing in 1979. China's Foreign Ministry urged Washington to handle the Taiwan question with 'utmost prudence' and stop sending 'wrong signals' to Taipei.Just days after Trump's high-profile Beijing summit with Xi Jinping, where the Chinese president warned that mishandling Taiwan could lead to 'clashes and even conflicts,' Trump is signaling a willingness to upend decades of diplomatic precedent. Taiwan's Foreign Ministry said Lai would be 'happy to talk,' emphasizing the island's commitment to peace and stability. Lai also stressed that purchases of US weapons remain essential for maintaining deterrence across the Taiwan Strait.Trump approved arms sales to Taiwan while simultaneously praising his relationship with Xi as 'amazing' — a pattern of mixed signals that has raised concerns in Taipei about the consistency of US support. He called a potential Taiwan deal 'a very good negotiating chip.'
Why it matters: The timing is deliberate. Trump is using Taiwan as leverage in a broader China strategy just days after projecting warmth with Xi in Beijing. The move tests whether Beijing's warnings carry consequences, and whether the US-Taiwan relationship can survive being treated as a bargaining chip in Washington's negotiations with the mainland.
■ The Bottom Line Six weeks into the ceasefire, the US-Iran conflict has settled into a diplomatic stalemate that extracts costs from those least able to influence its outcome. Pakistan is mediating but has no leverage. Iran is reviewing proposals but signals no urgency. Trump says he can wait. Meanwhile, Japan and South Korea burn through strategic reserves, Jordan intercepts missiles over cities it did not target, India's fertilizer supply chain faces disruption, and China secures passage rights for its own fleet while declining to help others. The FAO warns of a food crisis in 6-12 months. Trump's signal of a direct Taiwan call tests Beijing's tolerance just days after a summit designed to project stability. The gap between who pays and who decides has become the central fact of this war — and it is widening, not closing.

item.title: Strategic Brief: The Hour Before War: Trump Pauses Iran Strike as Gulf Allies Intervene
item.description:
President Trump revealed he was one hour from ordering fresh strikes on Iran before Gulf states persuaded him to pause. Tehran has submitted a new peace proposal, but the gap between US demands and Iranian terms remains wide.
► The Brinkmanship Pause
President Trump stated publicly on Tuesday that he had been 'an hour away' from ordering a new round of strikes on Iran before postponing the attack at the request of Gulf allies. The disclosure reveals just how close the region came to a major escalation. Trump simultaneously warned that the US may still need to strike again, while claiming Tehran is 'begging for a deal.'Oil prices dropped more than 1% on the news, but the relief may prove temporary. The US Strategic Petroleum Reserve drew a record 9.9 million barrels last week, pushing total reserves to 374 million barrels — the lowest since July 2024. The drawdown reflects both the physical disruption from the Hormuz crisis and hedging against future supply shocks.
Why it matters: The pause demonstrates that Gulf states retain meaningful leverage over US decision-making, but the underlying standoff remains unresolved. Trump's willingness to broadcast how close he came to attack suggests a strategy of maximum pressure — and the next pause may not materialize.
► Iran's New Proposal and the Sticking Points
Tehran has sent a new peace proposal to Washington, though its terms remain distant from US demands. According to sources, Iran's proposal includes demands for war reparations and the complete withdrawal of US troops from the region. Washington, by contrast, wants Iran to halt uranium enrichment for 20 years and ship out its existing stockpiles to the United States.Neither side is publicly bending. The economic pressure on Iran has intensified, but Tehran appears to be betting that Washington's appetite for prolonged conflict remains limited — especially with oil markets already volatile and Gulf allies pressing for de-escalation.
Why it matters: The core conflict is structural: Washington wants guarantees that Iran cannot quickly rebuild nuclear capabilities; Tehran wants an end to US military presence in its neighborhood. Neither demand is easily conceded, and the middle ground remains undefined.
► Putin's Beijing Visit: The Weakened Partner
Vladimir Putin arrived in Beijing on Tuesday for a two-day visit with Xi Jinping — his first major overseas trip since the Iran war escalated. The optics are deliberate: Putin follows Donald Trump's high-profile Beijing summit by less than a week, and Xi is receiving both leaders as China projects stability amid global turbulence.But the power dynamics have shifted. The New York Times analysis frames Putin as a 'weakened' leader now deeply 'in Xi's pocket.' Russia is seeking to deepen energy ties with China as Persian Gulf disruptions create opportunities for alternative suppliers. The Russian ruble hit a three-year high against the yuan ahead of the visit, reflecting increased trade flows. Yet Xi also reportedly warned Trump last week that Putin may come to regret his Ukraine invasion — a signal that Beijing is hedging its Russia bets.
Why it matters: China is positioning itself as the indispensable power — engaging both Washington and Moscow while maintaining optionality. Putin needs Chinese economic support more than ever, but Xi has little incentive to overcommit to a partner whose military position in Ukraine remains strained.
► The Trump-Putin Alaska Meeting
Donald Trump and Vladimir Putin are scheduled to meet face-to-face in Alaska on Friday. The agenda centers on finding pathways to end the war in Ukraine. The meeting follows weeks of diplomatic signaling, including Putin's recent peace overtures and Trump's public statements suggesting he wants to resolve the conflict.The Alaska venue is notable — neutral ground, far from either capital, echoing Cold War summit traditions. But the context is different: Putin arrives from Beijing in a dependent position, while Trump comes from a Middle East crisis where he has demonstrated willingness to use force. The power balance at the table has shifted.
Why it matters: A serious negotiation on Ukraine would require concessions from both sides. Putin may be calculating that Trump's desire for a deal — combined with the distraction of the Iran crisis — creates an opening. But Trump also needs to avoid appearing to reward Russian aggression while simultaneously pressing Iran.
► Economic Shockwaves Spread
Japan reported stronger-than-expected Q1 GDP growth on solid exports and consumption — but the data already feels like ancient history. The Iran war's energy shock has yet to fully filter through global supply chains. Japan, as a major energy importer, faces particular exposure. Analysts expect Q2 to show the real impact of sustained high oil prices and disrupted shipping routes.In the US, incoming Federal Reserve Chair Kevin Warsh has unsettled central banking peers by suggesting that Fed independence may not fully extend to its crisis-fighting role abroad. The implication — that the Fed might scale back its global dollar liquidity operations — has raised concerns about market stability precisely when the global financial system faces stress from energy price spikes and trade disruptions.
Why it matters: The Iran conflict is no longer a regional story. Energy shocks, supply chain disruptions, and policy uncertainty are propagating through the global economy. The combination of military brinkmanship in the Gulf and central bank policy shifts in Washington creates multiple channels for financial instability.
► Qatar's Diplomatic Position
Qatar publicly stated that no country has the right to block the Strait of Hormuz — a notable position from a state that has historically maintained good relations with Tehran. The statement reflects Gulf calculations that the costs of prolonged closure now outweigh any benefit of tacit support for Iranian actions. Qatar, along with other Gulf states, played a role in persuading Trump to pause the planned strike.The Gulf states are walking a narrow line: pressing Washington for de-escalation while also signaling to Tehran that their tolerance for regional disruption has limits. Their leverage comes from their role as alternative energy suppliers and as hosts for US military bases.
Why it matters: Gulf diplomacy is now a live variable in the Iran equation. These states are not passive observers — they are actively shaping US and Iranian calculations through both public statements and private interventions.
■ The Bottom Line The Iran standoff entered its most dangerous phase today: Trump was one hour from a major strike before Gulf allies secured a pause. Tehran has submitted a new proposal, but the gap between US demands (20-year enrichment halt, stockpile transfer) and Iranian terms (reparations, troop withdrawal) remains vast. Meanwhile, Putin visits Beijing in a weakened position before his Alaska summit with Trump on Friday, and economic shockwaves from the Gulf crisis are beginning to register across Asia and in central bank policy debates. The war has not ended — it has been paused, with the clock still running.

item.title: Strategic Brief: Putin-Xi Summit Follows Trump Visit as Great Power Competition Intensifies
item.description:
Just days after Trump's high-stakes Beijing summit, Putin arrives for talks with Xi as Russia strikes Chinese shipping near Ukraine and Ukraine launches its largest drone offensive of the war. Meanwhile, France pivots toward East Africa and G7 ministers coordinate sanctions strategy in Paris.
► Putin-Xi Summit Tests Beijing's Balancing Act
Vladimir Putin arrived in Beijing on Monday for talks with Xi Jinping, just four days after Donald Trump concluded a two-day state visit to China. The timing is deliberate: Xi is managing parallel relationships with Washington and Moscow while both relationships face strain from different directions.The Chinese readout from the Trump summit introduced a new framework — "constructive strategic stability" — with Xi warning that Taiwan remains the issue most likely to trigger conflict between the two powers. Xi stated bluntly that "Taiwan independence and cross-Strait peace are as irreconcilable as fire and water." Trump declined to commit to continued arms sales to Taiwan during the visit.Putin's visit comes amid growing friction: Russia struck a Chinese cargo vessel bound for Ukraine on Monday, damaging the ship and sparking a fire. The KSL DEYANG, flying under a Marshall Islands flag, was heading to Ukraine's Pivdennyi port to load iron ore when it was hit by a Russian drone. Zelensky noted that "the Russians could not have been unaware of what vessel was at sea."
Why it matters: Beijing is trying to maintain partnerships with both Washington and Moscow while avoiding direct confrontation with either. Striking Chinese shipping tests that balance — and creates awkward optics just as Putin seeks Xi's continued support.
► Ukraine Launches Largest Drone Offensive of the War
Russia intercepted 3,124 Ukrainian drones in the past week, including 1,054 destroyed on May 17 alone — one of the largest aerial assaults of the war. The scale represents a significant Ukrainian manufacturing and operational capacity that has survived Russian strikes on energy and defense infrastructure.Russia responded with a massive overnight barrage of its own, launching 524 drones and 22 missiles at Ukraine. The main target was Dnipro, where dozens were injured and residential buildings were damaged. Air defense units shot down or neutralized 503 drones and four missiles.The drone war has intensified as both sides target infrastructure. Russian forces struck Naftogaz energy facilities in the Dnipropetrovsk region, destroying a filling station and injuring two employees. Meanwhile, Ukrainian forces continue to pressure Russian border regions, with two killed in Belgorod from Ukrainian drone attacks.
Why it matters: The aerial war has entered a new phase of mutual bombardment. Ukraine is demonstrating it can sustain large-scale drone production despite Russian strikes, while Russia shows it can still overwhelm Ukrainian air defenses in certain sectors.
► G7 Ministers Coordinate Ukraine Support in Paris
Finance ministers from the G7 met in Paris on Monday to discuss ongoing support for Ukraine amid the intensified conflict. Ukraine's Finance Minister Serhii Marchenko called for increased sanctions pressure on Russia, while confirming that the first disbursement from the previously agreed 90 billion euro EU loan is expected in early June.European Economic Commissioner Valdis Dombrovskis reaffirmed the need to open the Strait of Hormuz, linking the Iran conflict to broader economic concerns. The dual focus on Ukraine and Iran reflects how the two conflicts are increasingly interconnected in Western strategic planning.The EU also announced new sanctions on May 11 against 16 individuals and seven entities responsible for the systematic unlawful deportation of Ukrainian children to Russia. The measures are part of an ongoing sanctions regime that continues to expand despite concerns about enforcement gaps.
Why it matters: Western financial and sanctions coordination continues even as attention splits between Ukraine and Iran. The 90 billion euro loan disbursement in June will be a critical test of whether EU support mechanisms remain functional at scale.
► France Pivots to East Africa with Kenya Summit
French President Emmanuel Macron arrived in Nairobi on Sunday ahead of the Africa Forward Summit, marking France's attempt to rebrand its relationship with the continent from post-colonial dominance to "partnership of equals." The summit, held for the first time in an Anglophone country, draws 30 heads of state and follows the complete withdrawal of French troops from West Africa last year.Kenya and France signed 11 agreements covering nuclear energy, transport modernization, and sustainable agriculture. Kenya's President William Ruto emphasized that Nairobi is "neither looking East nor West" but "looking forward," signaling continued African preference for non-aligned partnerships.Kenya's opposition leader Kalonzo Musyoka criticized the summit's timing, citing concerns about democracy and human rights ahead of the 2027 general election. The criticism highlights the domestic political constraints facing African leaders as they engage with former colonial powers.
Why it matters: France is attempting to rebuild African influence after losing its military foothold in the Sahel. The pivot to East Africa and Anglophone partners represents a strategic shift, but faces competition from other powers and domestic political resistance in host countries.
► Iran Tensions Simmer as Hormuz Crisis Enters Third Month
The Strait of Hormuz remains effectively closed to tanker traffic as the US-Iran standoff continues with no clear resolution timeline. The closure has pushed oil prices higher and forced major economies to draw down strategic reserves while exploring alternative supply routes.CNN reported that Iran is developing capabilities beneath the Strait, though details remain limited. The report suggests Tehran is positioning for a prolonged confrontation rather than seeking a quick diplomatic off-ramp.The conflict was a central topic during Trump's Beijing summit, with both the US and China seeking to manage the economic fallout. China remains a major purchaser of Iranian oil and has interests in seeing the strait reopened, while also opposing American military presence in the region.
Why it matters: The Hormuz closure is now a structural feature of global energy markets rather than a temporary disruption. The longer it persists, the more pressure builds on all sides for either escalation or negotiation.
► Hungary's Political Shift Reshapes EU Dynamics
Viktor Orbán's defeat in Hungary's April elections has removed Russia's most reliable advocate inside the European Union. The new TISZA government holds a supermajority and has signaled intent to align more closely with EU positions on Ukraine and sanctions.The shift matters for sanctions enforcement and EU enlargement talks. Orbán's government had served as a brake on multiple EU initiatives, from aid packages to Ukraine to the expansion of sanctions regimes. His removal changes the calculus for EU decision-making on Russia policy.While the transition is ongoing, early signals suggest the new government will not exercise the vetoes that characterized the Orbán era. This could accelerate EU support for Ukraine and streamline decision-making on security issues.
Why it matters: Moscow lost its most effective advocate inside the EU. The change removes a systematic obstacle to European coordination on Ukraine and sanctions, though the new government's full policy direction remains to be seen.
■ The Bottom Line Three great power summits in one week — Trump-Xi, then Putin-Xi — show how Beijing sits at the center of current geopolitical dynamics. China is managing relationships with both Washington and Moscow while trying to avoid entanglement in either conflict. The strike on Chinese shipping near Ukraine complicates that balancing act. Meanwhile, the Ukraine war has entered a new phase of mutual drone bombardment, and France is attempting to rebuild African influence after its Sahel retreat. The Hormuz closure continues to shape global energy markets with no resolution in sight.

item.title: Strategic Brief: Three Fronts Intensify: Ukraine Strikes Moscow, Ebola Spreads, and Iran's Demands Grow
item.description:
Ukraine launched its largest drone attack on Moscow in over a year as Pakistan shuttles new Iranian demands to Washington. A WHO-declared Ebola emergency in Congo and Uganda adds a global health crisis to an already strained international system.
► Ukraine's Major Drone Strike on Moscow
Ukraine launched its largest drone attack on the Russian capital in more than a year overnight, deploying nearly 600 drones that killed at least four people in the Moscow region and wounded a dozen others. Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky called the strikes "entirely justified" in response to deadly Russian attacks on Ukrainian cities.The attack marks an escalation in Ukraine's long-range strike capabilities and signals Kyiv's willingness to take the war deeper into Russian territory. Among the casualties was an Indian national working in the Moscow region, highlighting how the conflict's reach now extends beyond the immediate combatants to affect third-country civilians.
Why it matters: This strike demonstrates Ukraine's continued ability to project power deep into Russia despite ongoing pressure on the frontlines. It also complicates any prospects for renewed negotiations and could prompt Russian retaliation against Ukrainian cities.
► Pakistan's Mediation Push and Iran's Expanded Demands
Pakistan's Interior Minister Mohsin Naqvi arrived in Tehran on Saturday for a two-day unannounced visit aimed at reviving stalled US-Iran peace talks.
